title: Part 13
text: to choose the paths that I should follow. (Descartes, Discourse on Method) The general break with the mediaeval world has often been described as an ‘anthropological revolution’, placing man as the origin of meaning. This revolution occurred over several centuries affecting all the sub-domains of human thought and activity, and reformulating most basically the role of man in the production of truth, order and beauty. Breaking the ‘cosmological shell’ of the mediaeval universe implied a new emphasis on human creativity and human agency, and opened up new horizons of another man-made world. In philosophy this anthropological revolution can be identified as reaching its apex with Descartes and the Cartesian cogito, which on the standard view is seen as the beginning of modern philosophy, if not indeed of modern thought (Sorell 1997: xxix). If so, modern philosophy and thought originate with a very specific self-discovery that needs to be analysed in its own terms. One strong desire cuts across Descartes’ authorship: the need to undo a philosophy based on knowledge inherited from the past. We should not rely on ‘what others have thought’, as stated the third rule in Rules for the direction of the mind (Descartes 1997: 8). Descartes was here taking issue with the ancients, in particular with the scholastic tradition, but in general Descartes attacked customs and habits of the mind that people employed without use of proof-testing. He wanted to establish a method that could eliminate human errors from the chain of logical reasoning. In Discourse on Method (published in 1637), Descartes presented his scientific method, which was further developed in Meditations on First Philosophy, first published in Latin in 1641. Descartes’ philosophical trajectory can itself be analysed as a ritual passage. This is as implicitly argued in the short, suggestive Introduction to Descartes’ work by Chávez-Arvizo who likens Descartes’ metaphysical project to a ‘voyage’ of truth discovery undertaken by the nameless and timeless meditator (1997: xv). As stressed by van Gennep (1960) each step of the ritual passage (separation, liminality proper and re-integration) is subdivided into several rituals that test and ultimately transform the initiand who goes through the trial. The first stage of separation most often happens physically as the initiands are physically removed from their society, literally losing all hitherto existing reference points and prior knowledge. The tripartite structure is most clearly present in Meditations on First Philosophy. In the First Meditation Descartes starts his mental voyage by separating himself from all pre-established ‘opinions’; the method of doubt progressively takes apart all preconceived knowledge, starting by fundamentally doubting sense-based beliefs, doubting whether it is even possible to know whether one is asleep or awake, arriving at the utmost point of radical Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 121 doubt about the world: the heavens, the earth, the sounds and all ‘external things’ may be nothing but an illusion. In the Second Meditation Descartes overcomes the void and finds ‘something which is certain’, the cogito: the fact that I am thinking, therefore I exist. This new-gained certainty is immediately tested against further doubts. Descartes’ procedure here closely resembles the importance in ritual passages of proving one’s new identity through testing. The following meditations aim at testing the existence of God (the Third Meditation) and of material things (the Fifth Meditation). The Sixth Meditation establishes another truth, namely the distinction between mind and body, which is first tested and then established as a foundation that supports the cogito as belonging to the immortal mind. Descartes manages to come out at the other end of the passage with a newborn identity: a new Archimedean point of truth and knowledge, the fact that he is a thinking thing. He faces the entire gamut of un-truths that thrive within his world, and drives out the evil. The ritual passage is an individual voyage, but with universal implications: Descartes is simply laying bare a truth which is inbuilt in the universe, and kindly offers truth to the world. In fact, Descartes is both the initiand and the master of ceremony in the ritual passage. He establishes universal reason discovered in his individual self. When taken as a singular eruption of thought this sounds terribly like the idea of a madman, but the exercise had historical predecessors and a larger social field of emergence. Descartes and the Demon of Error Descartes’ bearing on the Christian atmosphere of seventeenth century Europe are very clear, and have been pointed out in the biographical literature. One major indirect impact was the general ‘internalization of religion’, produced by the Reformation and the Counter-Reformation (Gaukroger 1995: 25). The rival churches converged in the attempt to transform the church from public to private, to make faith a matter of personal internalized conviction. One of the tools was the generalized use of the ascetic technique of self-retreat, combined with the negative definition of the self as it had developed in the monasteries and as formulated in the idea of contemptus mundi that preached the hatred of the body and the world, the pervasiveness of sin and the fleetingness of time (Gaukroger 1995: 25). Descartes applied in converted form some of the ascetic techniques and contemptus mundi to himself and to his philosophy: he engaged in his own kind of therapeutic self-reform similar to the monastic experience; he hated the fluctuating truths of his perceptions, the weaknesses of his body, the un-truths which guided the world, the pervasiveness of error; and he had a sharp sense of the fleetingness of conceptual thought. Descartes’ own relationship with the church was full of tensions. Although he remained a confessional Catholic, he dismissed most of scholasticism, and attacked the Aristotelian philosophical premises that were then ruling Catholicism. This brought him into trouble with the Catholic Church, and therefore partly with his Liminality and the Modern 122 own Jesuit educational background. Descartes would constantly have to assure his Jesuit brothers that his rationalization of the world was not in contradiction with the teachings of the Church. Yet, as a resident in the Netherlands (from 1628–1649), Descartes’ ideas inevitably provoked those with Orthodox Calvinist ideas, and Descartes was not wise enough to stay out of trouble in his host country, where he eventually became looked upon with suspicion as well (Watson 2007: 221–43). However, beyond the substantial debates that brought Descartes into conflict with official doctrines (Catholic and Calvinist alike) one can detect an underlying religious inspiration to his problématique: Descartes’ problem was how to make statements about the existence of the world that can be taken as absolute truth. ‘Error’ to Descartes was a kind of evil, and Gellner (1992) is surely right in indicating how Descartes’ understanding of the problem was deeply religious in nature: what tormented Descartes was not that he might be damned, but that he might be mistaken (Gellner 1992: 13). Descartes’ language is on this point most direct in Meditations on First Philosophy, and especially in the Fourth Meditation, Of the true and the false, where he tackles the problem of ‘error’. Descartes talks about the will that ‘easily falls into error and sin, and chooses the evil for the good, or the false for the true’ (1997: 166), putting thus truth and good, and error and evil on the same plane. True enough, in his own introduction to his Meditations (ibid.: 123–33), Descartes is keen to stress (before his Catholic audience) that the ‘sin’ he will talk about relates only to errors in deciding between the true and false, and not to questions of faith (ibid.: 132); yet his drawing on the same vocabulary is at the same stroke further underlined; and Descartes, as we know, will not refrain from proving the existence of God. This drove Descartes to the question: why did God put error into the world? The question is identical to another question, which God’s children normally address from an early age: why did God put evil into the world? If God decides everything, why is there evil? The believer is puzzled and arrives at the conclusion that either evil was some kind of secret force that slipped out of God’s control (Satan as the jealous angel), or that God (since He is, after all, almighty) let evil loose to punish mankind. Descartes found no satisfaction in either answer. Indeed, he found such answers part of the error he wanted to undo. He proved that error had not been God’s intention at all. Error had come about by an erroneous path taken towards cognitive truth, and in his Fourth Meditation Descartes claims to have set us back on the right track of true reason (ibid.: 166–9). Having singled out error as the demon, Descartes wanted to establish some ground on which error was defeated by truth. That ground was his self. From there, Descartes went on to redesign himself out of a solitary vision. His road to liberation went through a refuge into self, and from this refuge he could reach salvation from error. Knowledge saves. Although one should not take such statements at face value, Descartes himself claimed that from an early age he received signs concerning some kind of revelation of truth, that he would dedicate his life to the pursuit of truth, and that his possible success in this self-imposed task would serve as a new basis of universal order and knowledge (see Rodis-Lewis 1992). Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 123 Descartes and the New Ground of Order A few further points about Descartes and his new philosophy must be made explicit at this point. Descartes can surely be considered a Weberian ‘switchman’: he is one of those thinkers who managed to change the shape of the subject before him. Weber invoked the metaphor of switchmen in the context of his comparative study of the world religions (1948), invoking the prophet as ideal type. Weber never worked out a full-blown typology for intellectual figures, but in so many ways a thinker like Descartes performs as a prophet: he diagnoses the illnesses of his age, establishes a new truth through inward reflexivity, and then offers the truth to the world as a revelatory key to a new epoch. He posits himself as the medium through which the entire world can bring about a change. The radical nature of Descartes’ philosophy is visible by the way in which he ‘clears the ground’ by totally erasing the relevance of pre-existing knowledge and experience. In The Passions of the Soul Descartes characteristically declared that he feels obliged to write ‘as though I were treating of a matter which no one had ever touched on before me’ (1997: 358). Descartes’ attack on almost all existing knowledge is repeated elsewhere. In The Principles of Philosophy, first published in 1644, Descartes dismantled what he here called the ‘prejudices of childhood’ (1997: 305), the source of all errors: our reliance on sense-impressions. And also here Descartes proceeded to replace the world of errors with a new-found certainty, the cogito. The gnostic thinker creates a void so that he can erect a new edifice from scratch and bestow order on the world – the whole world. How can one understand Descartes’ ‘ordering experience’? The exact road taken by Descartes towards pure reason is not our issue here. It went through doubt, and it went through absolute divisions of body and mind, faith and reason. In Discourse on Method, Descartes had argued that the self was ‘completely distinct from the body’ and could exist without it (Descartes 1999: 25). Paving the way for the cogito, this was the much known and criticized mind-body dualism that Descartes installed. Indeed, Descartes was openly taking departure from the Aristotelian account of soul which denied the mental any reification as separate substance. In this sense, Descartes returned to Plato’s notion of the soul as separate from the body, while at the same time severely delimiting what ‘soul’ means. 4 To Plato, the soul was composed of logos, thymos and eros, and humans should strive towards reaching a balance between these dimensions of reason, emotion 4 Descartes’ thoughts on the soul must again be understood against the background of the seventeenth century debates about ‘passions’ and how to account for them. In The Passions of the Soul, Descartes distinguishes in articles 24 and 25 between passions which relate to the body (like heat and cold) and passions which relate to the soul, i.e. phenomena like perceptions and emotions which are ‘moved by spirits’ (article 26). But in order to remove any hint of extra-physiological reasoning about feelings and spirits, in article 31 Descartes states that the soul is situated in the pineal gland. Liminality and the Modern 124 and desire. Aristotle equally argued for a composite nature of the human soul encompassing rational and emotive dimensions. But this composite and multi- layered anima was exactly what Descartes wanted to get rid of. 5 Descartes’ real break with Aristotle was therefore not just the placing of the self outside body, but also the turning of the self into mind. The self-reform that Descartes brought about was directed exclusively towards logos: his finding of truth was a matter of knowledge as it can be established on the basis of the individual rational mind, and on nothing outside of that. 6 Therefore, the ‘inside’ that Descartes explored and then publicly declared was of a very special quality. His philosophy was a tearing apart of components, an outstanding example of maximum orderliness and analytic capacity to strip matters down to their smallest components: an exercise in anatomy. It is reason as dry and soulless as can be, as should be. The order that Descartes found was an order of the mind; the truth he found was a truth of the thinking self. Descartes’ Prophecy This is a first radical difference from religious prophecy, as discussed by Weber (1978: 439–67). The self to Descartes has nothing to do with soul in either the ancient Greek or Christian traditions. In the latter, the notion of the soul, in spite of historical developments of the concept, has always been connected to transcendental experiences, without which we do not become really human. Whenever Descartes discusses the word ‘soul’ he uses it as a synonym for ‘mind’ or ‘thought’, that which goes on in the brain. Before Descartes it was commonplace to regard the soul the animating principle of the human person, in contrast to the body understood as a biological mechanism. In The Passions of the Soul, Descartes states with medical certainty 7 that the soul operates from a ‘certain very small gland’ situated in the middle of the brain (articles 31 and 32; in Descartes 1997: 372). The mind may be separate from the body, but its functioning can be approached with a mechanistic, naturalistic-deductive model. 5 Although in Part II of The Passions of the Soul Descartes lists six primitive passions (wonder, love, hate, desire, joy and sadness), he is very consciously rejecting any real distinction between parts of the soul, such as the Platonic distinction between the rational, concupiscible and irascible appetities. For a fuller discussion, see Gaukroger (1995: 384–417). 6 Descartes represents, also in this important sense, a rupture with the use of meditative practices as developed in the philosophical traditions of late antiquity and early Christianity (as in Augustine). Descartes’ meditations are exactly not spiritual (following the work by Hadot 1995), as they confine the scope of human searching to cognitive sensing and logical operations upon primary concepts and ideas. 7 Descartes’ interest in anatomy and physiology goes back to the late 1620s. He studied dissected animal bodies, and in a letter to Mersenne from 1632 he tells that having investigated the body’s life-functions, he is now successfully dissecting animal heads to explain mental phenomena like imagination and memory (Gaukroger 1995: 227–8). Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 125 Descartes’ system therefore produces a crucial narrowing of the problem of being, rather than – as prophecy normally provokes – a differentiation of soul via a differentiation of consciousness and human experiences. 8 Mind may provide the basis of an ordered universe, but human experiences outside the mind remain undifferentiated and unordered. From Descartes onwards the self becomes mind. The mind rejects the environment and finds its path forwards through thoughtful reflection upon itself. Descartes claimed to have separated the evils of his day, which were the unreliable nature of bodily sensation, of mental habit and tradition. The Cartesian cogito resulted in a belief in, and practice of, self as the origin of knowledge and truth that came to dominate Western philosophy. It is of course true that Kant and Hume both claimed to have refuted Descartes, but to an important extent they carried on the premises of the problem, namely that the sources of truth and untruth were somehow located in the self. Here lies a second difference from traditional prophecy: Descartes’ truth- revelation did not start with a transcendental vision. It is from the existence of the thinking self, the cogito, that Descartes proved the existence of God. The structure of revelation goes from the solitary self as the basis of thinking to the existence of God. Descartes argued deductively that since he as a human being with a body was not perfect but still had the idea of perfection, that idea must exist somewhere. That somewhere, he claimed in Discourse on Method, was God (Descartes 1999: 26). The grace he found, even as to the existence of God, was purely cognitive. Descartes’ vision is not a vision of divinity from whom human thinking and reason finds its place. It goes the other way round: human thinking is the vision into which divinity must enter, and it enters exactly insofar as it fulfils the conditions of logical reason. If Descartes is ‘the philosopher of modernity’ it cannot be because he clears new ground for safe knowledge (even when taken on its own premises his theory was definitively refuted by Kant). Descartes should rather be considered the founder of modern philosophy in the sense that he is the outstanding exemplification of the modern person who scrutinizes his self in order to devise system and reason in the rationalized world. In this sense Descartes was an exemplary ‘Protestant’ of the generic Weberian type, 9 using the ascetic techniques of withdrawal, reflection and self-perfection for this-worldly purposes of truth and order. 10 But Descartes 8 One can here refer to another central concept in Voegelin’s approach, namely ‘differentiation of consciousness’. Importantly, however, Voegelin identified two types of differentiation, noetic and pneumatic, working through mind and soul, and with the decisive differentiations taking place in the Hellenic and Judaeo-Christian traditions respectively. Using a Voegelinian language, one might risk the formulation that the noetic differentiation (and its real-world effects) that follows from Cartesian thought dangerously predicates pneumatic undifferentiation. 9 In separating faith from reason Descartes was in fact reinforcing, at the epistemological level, a tendency initiated by the Protestant reformers: the separation between faith and action. 10 Perhaps not too much should be made of it, but The Society of Jesus was formed as the principal arm of the Counter-Reformation, and exactly in that quality it had taken on Liminality and the Modern 126 is not only the philosopher of modernity because he locates truth and knowledge in the self, and because of his meditative path to truth. Both moves have their historical significance but they had been made before. If those were the criteria, stoicism should easily be recognized as the first truly modern philosophy. Despite all the obvious affinities, we generally hesitate to make this claim. Why? The answer is that Descartes is the philosopher of modernity because he is the first to systematically apply ascetic techniques to work on mind instead of soul, and because he is the first to apply the powers so gained to achieve system and mathematical precision in the de-Divinized this-worldly realm. In his discussion of Descartes, Borkenau shows how Descartes managed to offer a proper solution to the anxieties of the age, not at the level of philosophical or spiritual substance, but rather at the level of the ‘mood’ [Stimmung] that underlies a world image. Descartes is the philosopher of modernity because he reduces human existence to a cognitive challenge of positive knowledge established through work on the self as mind. And it is in this sense that the modern world is indeed Cartesian to the core (Watson 2007: 3). Fear and Order: The Hobbesian Self and the Hobbesian Sovereign Whosoever looketh into himself ... (Hobbes, Leviathan) If we could conceive the world annihilated except one man to whom there would remain ideas or images ... (Hobbes, drafts for De Corpore, quoted in Tuck) Even if Cartesian thinking quickly became an object of dispute, and even if several of his ideas were later falsified, his formulation of the general epistemological problem of Western philosophy would remain. From an anthropological perspective, cause for further wonder ought to reside in the question of the precise nature of the historical moment at which the question could ever become so paramount and thus formulated: how can the thinking self be the basis of universal truth? How can we, from within our own resources, arrive at some justifiable secure knowledge of the world? Fichte would phrase the pre-occupation well when he defined the very task of philosophy as a task to discover ‘the absolutely many of the educational principles that had given the Reformation its formidable successes. This implied a heavy stress on the internalization of religion, a systematic and rule-bound conduct, and also the idea that each person, high or low, should be treated equally before God. In order to shape the will of the person, Ignatius of Loyola, the founder of the movement, applied a soldier-like discipline of minute control of behaviour and constant supervision to the Jesuit institutions, and it was in such an environment, at the Jesuit School La Flèche, that Descartes had his primary education (see Gaukroger 1995: 38–65). It has often been argued that Descartes adopted his stoic, dissecting morality and his pragmatic this-worldly orientation here (see for example Watson 2007: 65–77). Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 127 unconditioned first principle of human knowledge’, a task he identified as the Vocation of Man (Fichte 1961). However, this search for first principles was equally important for modern political thought. Thomas Hobbes’ problem of order was indeed similar in kind: what plagued Hobbes was how to establish, in the midst of the reigning disorder, some absolutely unconditioned first principle, not only of human knowledge, but also of political rule and morality. 11 Moreover, Hobbes’ answers ran in certain important respects parallel to those offered by Descartes. Hobbes’ problem can be described in similar terms as those introduced above. Europe had been in a prolonged state of war for almost half a century. His political writings emerged ‘in a period of intense political turmoil’ (Dietz 1990a: 1). During the Thirty Years’ War, Hobbes saw how the schismatic churches shed blood with the intent of establishing a secular order on the basis of their inwardly-felt religious claims to truth. It was exactly this use of religious truth for political order that Hobbes found both erratic and dangerous. Hobbes’ fear of passionate religious inner-driven sentiments was understandable, considering the historical context and the Puritan revolutions in England. Different varieties of moral philosophy and transcendental truth kept defending different varieties of legitimate public order, and the result was bloody, to say the least. As Hobbes says in his 1641 Epistle dedicatory to De Cive, ‘the war of the sword and the war of the pens is perpetual’ (in Tuck and Silverthorne 1998: 5), and to Hobbes both types of war were causally related. There was a need to clean up the mess, to find some ground from where truth could be established in order for peace to prevail. This was Hobbes’ problem. Christianity had lost its function as a non-disputed civil theology; its powers had been exhausted, that much was clear. Something else was needed, and Hobbes claimed to have found it. The contract theories of the seventeenth century aimed to organize society according to the parameters of rational thought established in philosophy and science. What should also be considered, however, is the kind of human self that was institutionalized alongside this process. As was custom among the contract theorists, Hobbes would typically devote the first section of his reasoning to a discussion of man and human nature. The geometrical grid that Hobbes pursued required basic definitions. This first definitional part, then, directed the political analysis and its programmatic results. The implicit idea, still dominant in much political theory and ideology, is that once we know and can declare human nature as, for example, either individualistic or collective, peaceful or warlike, we also know how to govern. This construct is inherently blind to another possibility: that ‘human nature’ is likewise a product of rule and power. 11 The epistemological status of this ‘first principle’ in Hobbes’ philosophy is not our issue here; but it should of course be stressed that Hobbes did not claim to have found absolute truth as did Descartes. He did claim, however, to have singled out the cause-effect relationships as they functioned in the empirical world, and he also claimed to have a good idea (a ‘first principle’) for improving them. Liminality and the Modern 128 The immediate parallels with Descartes are clear enough in Hobbes’ main work, Elementa Philosophiae, which was composed of the three sections, De Corpore, De Homine and De Cive (although the three pieces were not published together under this title until 1668). First of all, Hobbes started out his entire work by repeating Descartes’ radical doubt about the existence of the world: the world and its magnitudes, motions, sounds and colours appear as if they are external objects even if they are in fact ‘phantasms internally happening’ (Hobbes from De Corpore, translated and quoted by Tuck 1998: xv). Hobbes argued that there is no way in which we can really know anything about the external world: we are simply imagining an external world through our ‘internal phantasms’; on this point Hobbes may have been under the direct influence of Descartes (see Tuck 2002). But if we cannot know about the world, then we can direct our investigation to that ‘inner reality’ which is the self, or human nature. It was here that Hobbes substantially disagreed with Descartes concerning the nature of the human – as he disagreed with Descartes’ proof of the existence of God and the possibility that this ‘proof’ could inject veracity into our sense-impressions. At the same time, the two arguments are also very similar: we cannot know about the external world, so let us turn the gaze inwards. Establish from within an unquestionable truth, and construct a system of order that flows from there towards the world. The projects are readily comparable as acts of world-making and forms of order-bestowing. Hobbes’ First Principles What, then was Hobbes’ axiom? This he stated very clearly in the first edition of De Cive, published in Paris in 1642, when Hobbes had already fled to Paris, scared by the prospects of civil war. Presenting the essence of his work in the Epistle dedicatory to De Cive, Hobbes claimed that ‘I obtained two absolutely certain postulates of human nature’, namely ‘human greed’ and ‘natural reason, by which each man strives to avoid violent death as the supreme evil in nature’ (Hobbes in Tuck and Silverthorne 1998: 6). Hobbes’ first principle, his ‘uncaused cause’, was the principle of self- preservation: the right for each man to protect his life and limbs as far as he can. It was important for Hobbes to stress that this foundation was a natural right and therefore just (De Cive, 1.7, in ibid.: 27). To Hobbes, this political principle of self-preservation could likewise function as a foundation for ethics. In De Cive Hobbes launched yet another attack on the ancients, which was here directed not only against their ‘naïve realism’ but specifically against the Aristotelian idea that man is an animal ‘born fit for society’. No, says Hobbes, we are not born to live together in peace and harmony. If people seek each other’s company they do it to maximize personal honour, to gain a personal advantage, or to enjoy their own glory. The logical conclusion followed that ‘the origin of large and lasting societies lay not in mutual human benevolence but in men’s mutual fear’ (Hobbes in ibid.: 24). Hobbes, of course, did insist on linking political order to more noble Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 129 civic virtues, such as gratitude (see Dietz 1990b), but such virtues could only be re-injected while based upon a recognition of the true human nature. Hobbes famously restates his notion of the self in his most well-known work, Leviathan, finished in the aftermath of the Treaty of Westphalia, and ‘occasioned by the disorders of the present time’, as Hobbes says in his conclusion, with a clear reference to the English civil war (Hobbes 1968: 728). 12 The human person from which Hobbes built his model is a passionate individual, isolated in its origins, and motivated primarily by its passions for power: ‘I put for a generall inclination of all mankind, a perpetuall and restlesse desire of Power after power, that ceaseth onely in death’ (Leviathan, Part I, Chapter XI; here quoted from Hobbes 1968: 161). The Hobbesian person possessed an essentially unordered, anarchic self. Therefore, argued Hobbes, the common good of society cannot be established on the basis of this self. Here, of course, Hobbes was deeply anti-Cartesian. 13 However, he nonetheless established a different kind of first principle within the human self. What may eventually bring individuals to reason is fear: fear of death, fear of annihilation. This mutual fear, for Hobbes, was the only ‘positive’ passion with which society could be held together. In so doing, Hobbes took a dangerous type of human being for granted as the normal human type, and devised the Leviathan
